evidence.quote_or_summary: As we approach July, the monsoons are in full swing. The constant downpours make sections of the trail (see difficult sections) prone to landslides and rockfalls. The trek becomes a risky proposition to undertake.

The region experiences pre-winter snows sometime in October. More so at higher elevation campsites like Bhojbasa and above. So, after mid-October, the weather makes trekking untenable.

values: Snow on trail and natural snow bridges over Bhagirathi in early summer
Receding snow and sparse vegetation turning green by mid-summer
Swelled Bhagirathi river from melting snow and pre-monsoon rains

Deeper blue, gentler-flowing Bhagirathi in autumn
Clear skies and stable weather in autumn
Excellent views of the entire valley in autumn

evidence.quote_or_summary: In early summers, you will find snow along the trail. This is also the time when you cross the Bhagirathi river over the natural snow bridges. The snow remains till Gaumukh and sometimes even beyond until the mid of May.

But by mid-summer (late May-early June), you will find the final traces of snow. But you’ll start noticing the sparse vegetation of the valley. The little vegetation that you find on the trail (most of which lies on the way to Chirbasa) starts turning green.
At this time, the melting snow adds to the swell of the Bhagirathi river that begins to flow with its full might. You’ll notice this at Suryakund itself in Gangotri.
The pre-monsoon rains in the valley only add to the force of the river.

Autumn also brings colours to the trail. The vegetation en route to Chirbasa turns into shades of yellows, browns and maroons. The Bhagirathi too turns a deeper shade of blue. Its flow is gentler compared to summers.
Snow/Rain: The weather is stable with clear skies. Low chances of precipitation.
This is the best season to do the Gaumukh Tapovan trek for excellent views of the entire valley.

Autumn also brings colours to the trail. The vegetation en route to Chirbasa turns into shades of yellows, browns and maroons. The Bhagirathi too turns a deeper shade of blue. Its flow is gentler compared to summers.
But by mid-summer (late May-early June), you will find the final traces of snow. But you’ll start noticing the sparse vegetation of the valley.

evidence.quote_or_summary: The constant downpours make sections of the trail (see difficult sections) prone to landslides and rockfalls. The trek becomes a risky proposition to undertake.
The trail to Tapovan though is untenable in monsoons.
The region experiences pre-winter snows sometime in October. More so at higher elevation campsites like Bhojbasa and above. So, after mid-October, the weather makes trekking untenable.

The summer season is not just popular among trekkers, but also among pilgrims. It is the busiest season for the Chardham Yatra. Therefore expect a lot of pilgrims on the trail until Gaumukh. This flow of pilgrims heading to the Gaumukh glacier continues even in the monsoon months of July and August.
Compared to summer, the autumn season on the Gaumukh Tapovan trail is shorter. It’s barely 4-5 weeks long.

values: Trekkers drawn to trekking in snow (summer)
Excellent valley views (autumn)
Experiencing autumn colours and stable clear skies (autumn)
Trekkers preferring fewer crowds (autumn)

evidence.quote_or_summary: Summer is a popular time to do the Gaumukh Tapovan trek, especially for trekkers who are drawn to trekking in snow.
This is the best season to do the Gaumukh Tapovan trek for excellent views of the entire valley.
Snow/Rain: The weather is stable with clear skies. Low chances of precipitation.
Tapovan is generally not as popular with pilgrims and you’ll notice lesser crowds in autumn.
